rationale: Art.20(1) requires management bodies to approve cybersecurity risk-management measures and oversee implementation; §38(1) BSIG mirrors this exactly, obligating management boards ('Geschäftsleitungen') to implement and oversee the §30 risk-management measures.

Art.20(1) third sentence makes management bodies liable for infringements; §38(2) BSIG establishes personal civil liability of management boards under applicable corporate law for breaches of §38(1), adding that waivers of damage claims are invalid — slightly stricter than NIS2 baseline.

Art.20(1) preserves national liability rules for public institutions; §38(1) BSIG applies to both private and public 'Einrichtungen', relying on existing sector-specific public-law liability regimes as the carve-out.

Art.20(2) requires Member States to mandate training for management body members; §38(3) BSIG makes this a direct legal obligation for Geschäftsleitungen covering risk identification, risk-management practices, and service-impact assessment — co-extensive with the Directive's three competence areas.

Art.20(2) second sentence encourages (but does not mandate) employee training; §30(2) Nr.7 BSIG elevates this to a mandatory measure requiring 'grundlegende Schulungen und Sensibilisierungsmaßnahmen' — Germany gold-plates by making employee awareness training obligatory, not merely encouraged.

Art.21(1) requires 'appropriate and proportionate technical, operational and organisational measures' using an all-hazards approach; §30(1) BSIG transposes this verbatim as 'geeignete, verhältnismäßige und wirksame technische und organisatorische Maßnahmen' with identical proportionality criteria (risk exposure, size, cost, probability, impact).

Art.21(1) requires measures to minimize impact on recipients and other services; §30(1) BSIG covers the same objective of preventing disruptions and minimizing security incident impacts to IT systems and processes.

Art.21(2)(a) mandates 'policies for risk analysis and information system security'; §30(2) Nr.1 BSIG transposes this as 'Konzepte in Bezug auf die Risikoanalyse und auf die Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik' — content and scope are co-extensive.
Art.21(2)(b) requires 'incident handling' procedures; §30(2) Nr.2 BSIG mandates 'Bewältigung von Sicherheitsvorfällen' — direct 1:1 transposition of incident management obligations.

Art.21(2)(c) covers 'business continuity, such as backup management and disaster recovery, and crisis management'; §30(2) Nr.3 BSIG transposes as 'Aufrechterhaltung des Betriebs, wie Backup-Management und Wiederherstellung nach einem Notfall, und Krisenmanagement' — verbatim equivalence.

Art.21(2)(d) requires supply chain security 'including security aspects of the relationships between each entity and its direct suppliers or service providers'; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG mirrors this as 'Sicherheit der Lieferkette einschließlich sicherheitsbezogener Aspekte der Beziehungen zu unmittelbaren Anbietern oder Diensteanbietern' — co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(e) mandates 'security in network and information systems acquisition, development and maintenance'; §30(2) Nr.5 BSIG transposes as 'Sicherheitsmaßnahmen bei Erwerb, Entwicklung und Wartung von informationstechnischen Systemen, Komponenten und Prozessen' — scope is equivalent, Germany adds explicit mention of 'components'.

Art.21(2)(f) requires 'policies and procedures to assess the effectiveness of cybersecurity risk-management measures'; §30(2) Nr.6 BSIG transposes as 'Konzepte und Verfahren zur Bewertung der Wirksamkeit von Risikomanagementmaßnahmen' — direct equivalence.

Art.21(2)(g) covers 'basic cyber hygiene practices and cybersecurity training'; §30(2) Nr.7 BSIG transposes as 'grundlegende Schulungen und Sensibilisierungsmaßnahmen im Bereich der Sicherheit' — direct equivalence for the operational training obligation.

Art.21(2)(h) requires 'policies and procedures regarding the use of cryptography and, where appropriate, encryption'; §30(2) Nr.8 BSIG transposes as 'Konzepte und Prozesse für den Einsatz von kryptographischen Verfahren' — encryption is subsumed under 'kryptographische Verfahren', scope is equivalent.

Art.21(2)(i) requires 'human resources security, access control policies and asset management'; §30(2) Nr.9 BSIG transposes as 'Konzepte für die Sicherheit des Personals, die Zugriffskontrolle und für die Verwaltung von Informationstechniksystemen' — all three components are co-extensive.

Art.21(2)(j) mandates MFA or continuous authentication, secured voice/video/text communications, and secured emergency communication systems; §30(2) Nr.10 BSIG transposes all three as 'Verwendung von Lösungen zur Multi-Faktor-Authentifizierung oder kontinuierlichen Authentifizierung, gesicherte Kommunikation und gesicherte Notfallkommunikation' — verbatim equivalence.

Art.21(3) adds a specific obligation to evaluate supplier vulnerabilities, security practices, and coordinated risk assessments per Art.22(1) when selecting measures; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG covers supply chain security broadly but does not separately enumerate the Art.21(3) evaluative methodology — Art.21(3) is a sub-obligation subsumed within but more specific than BSIG Nr.4.

Art.21(4) requires entities to implement all necessary corrective measures without undue delay upon non-compliance finding; §30(1) BSIG establishes the primary obligation but does not contain an explicit corrective-measures sub-provision — enforcement is addressed via §61-62 BSIG supervisory powers rather than an entity-facing corrective duty.

Art.21(5) authorises Commission implementing acts specifying technical requirements for DNS, cloud, data centres, CDNs, MSPs, and trust service providers; §30(3)-(5) BSIG expressly implements this by establishing that EU implementing acts take precedence over national requirements for these entity types, with residual ministerial regulation authority.

Art.21(6) (recital-level guidance) encourages use of European and international standards; §30(8)-(9) BSIG provides a formal mechanism for industry associations to propose sector-specific standards that BSI validates — Germany operationalises the ENISA-guidelines encouragement into a structured standards-validation process, which is broader than a direct transposition.

Art.21(6) allows Member States to require use of certified ICT products/services per ENISA cybersecurity certification schemes; §30(6) BSIG implements this by allowing regulations to require that only certified products/services under EU Regulation 2019/881 schemas be used — direct transposition.

Art.22(1) tasks the Cooperation Group (not individual entities) to carry out coordinated security risk assessments of critical ICT supply chains; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG imposes entity-level supply chain security obligations but does not transpose the Union-level cooperative assessment process — this is an institutional gap, covered only indirectly via Art.21(3) reference.

Art.22(2) authorises the Commission to identify specific critical ICT services/systems/products for risk assessment; §30(3)-(5) BSIG acknowledges EU-level supremacy for implementing acts but does not separately transpose the identification process — Germany relies on EU-level execution of Art.22(2) directly.

Art.23(1) requires entities to notify the CSIRT or competent authority without undue delay of significant incidents; §32(1) Nr.1 BSIG implements this as mandatory notification to a joint BSI/BBK notification centre within 24 hours, which is the initial notification stage — equivalent obligation and channel.

Art.23(1) second sentence requires entities to inform service recipients of incidents likely to affect them adversely; §35(1) BSIG transposes this as a BSI-ordered notification obligation requiring entities to promptly inform service recipients, with option for website publication — equivalent in scope, Germany adds BSI-order trigger as implementation mechanism.

Art.23(2) defines significant incidents as those causing severe operational disruption or financial loss to the entity; §32(1) BSIG adopts the same threshold of 'erheblicher Sicherheitsvorfall' (significant security incident), deferring detailed criteria to BSI procedure rules under §32(4).

Art.23(2)(b) extends significance to incidents causing considerable material or non-material damage to other persons; §32(1) BSIG's 'erheblicher Sicherheitsvorfall' concept encompasses this broader damage criterion, consistent with recital-level interpretation under German administrative practice.

Art.23(3) requires intermediate status reports upon request by the CSIRT or competent authority; §32(1) Nr.3 BSIG transposes as 'Zwischenmeldungen auf Anforderung des Bundesamtes' — identical on-demand reporting mechanism.

Art.23(4)(a) specifies early warning within 24 hours including suspected unlawful origin or cross-border impact; §32(1) Nr.1 BSIG transposes verbatim as notification within 24 hours including indication of suspected malicious origin and cross-border dimension — direct equivalence.

Art.23(4)(b) requires an incident notification within 72 hours with initial severity assessment and indicators of compromise; §32(1) Nr.2 BSIG mirrors this as detailed notification within 72 hours with severity assessment and compromise indicators — verbatim equivalence.
Art.23(4)(c) lists intermediate reports on request as part of the reporting sequence; §32(1) Nr.3 BSIG implements the same on-request status update mechanism — direct transposition.

Art.23(4)(d) requires a final report within one month covering detailed description, threat type, mitigation, and cross-border impact; §32(1) Nr.4 BSIG implements identical final report within one month with the same content requirements — direct equivalence.

Art.23(4) allows substituting a progress report for the final report when an incident is ongoing; §32(2) BSIG implements this as an explicit 'Fortschrittsmeldung' in lieu of the Abschlussbericht for ongoing incidents — direct equivalence.

Art.23(4)(e) adds a specific 24-hour deadline for trust service providers (eIDAS); §32(1) Nr.1 BSIG establishes the general 24-hour deadline for all covered entities but does not separately enumerate trust service providers — the sector-specific rule is subsumed in the general BSIG provision.

Art.23(5) requires the CSIRT or authority to provide initial feedback within 24 hours of receiving the early warning with guidance on mitigation; §36 BSIG establishes a Federal Office feedback obligation to reporting entities including guidance — equivalent obligation on the authority side.

Art.23(6) requires the CSIRT or authority to inform other affected Member States and ENISA of cross-border incidents; §32(5) BSIG transposes this as the Federal Office immediately forwarding incident reports to competent federal supervisory authorities — equivalent information-sharing mechanism, though Germany's formulation focuses on domestic authority sharing as the implementation step.

Art.23(7) allows authorities to require public disclosure where public awareness is necessary; §35(1) BSIG empowers BSI to order entities to notify service recipients or publish on their website — equivalent public-interest disclosure mechanism.

Art.23(8) requires the single point of contact to forward notifications to other affected Member States' SPoCs; §40 BSIG designates the Federal Office as both national liaison and central notification point (nationale Verbindungsstelle), fulfilling the SPoC forwarding function.

Art.23(9) requires SPoCs to submit quarterly anonymised summary reports to ENISA; §40 BSIG establishes the SPoC function and reporting to European bodies but does not separately specify the quarterly ENISA reporting frequency — this cadence obligation derives directly from the Directive rather than a BSIG sub-provision.

Art.23(10) requires CSIRTs to share incident information about CER-Directive critical entities with the relevant competent authorities; §32(5) BSIG implements general authority-sharing for incident reports but does not specifically cross-reference the CER Directive (2022/2557) critical entity category — partial transposition, related but not co-extensive.

Art.23(11) authorises Commission implementing acts on significance criteria for DNS, cloud, CDNs, MSPs; §32(4) BSIG empowers the Federal Office to establish notification procedure details and publishes them online — Germany's delegation to BSI administrative rules implements the significance-criteria mechanism, with EU implementing acts taking precedence per §30(3) by analogy.

Art.21(3) requires entities to consider results of Art.22(1) coordinated supply chain risk assessments when selecting measures; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG mandates supply chain security broadly but contains no explicit entity-level obligation to incorporate Cooperation Group assessment results — the EU-level coordination dimension is a subset not directly transposed at entity level.

Art.20(2) specifies training must enable members to identify risks and assess cybersecurity risk-management practices and their impact; §38(3) BSIG reproduces these three competence areas verbatim ('Erkennung und Bewertung von Risiken', 'Risikomanagementpraktiken', 'Auswirkungen auf Dienste') — precise equivalence.

Art.21(2)(d) second clause specifically requires assessing supplier security practices including secure development procedures; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG includes 'sicherheitsbezogene Aspekte der Beziehungen zu unmittelbaren Anbietern' which encompasses supplier security practice assessment — co-extensive at this sub-granularity.

Art.21(2)(e) includes 'handling and disclosure of vulnerabilities' within secure acquisition/development/maintenance; §30(2) Nr.5 BSIG explicitly adds 'einschließlich des Managements und der Offenlegung von Schwachstellen' (vulnerability management and disclosure) — Germany transposes this sub-element explicitly.

Art.23(1) in conjunction with Art.30 permits voluntary notification of incidents, cyber threats, and near-misses; §32 BSIG focuses exclusively on mandatory reporting of significant incidents — Germany does not transpose the voluntary near-miss notification pathway in §32, creating a source superset (NIS2 is broader at this sub-point).

Art.21(1) states measures shall be without prejudice to sector-specific Union legal acts; §30(3)-(5) BSIG implements this by explicitly conferring precedence on EU Commission implementing acts for DNS, cloud, CDN, MSP and trust service provider entity types — direct transposition.

NIS2 Art.21 does not separately distinguish KRITIS operators; §31(1) BSIG gold-plates by imposing enhanced proportionality standards for Betreiber kritischer Anlagen, considering consequences of failure of the specific critical installation — Germany adds a KRITIS-specific tier beyond NIS2's uniform baseline.

NIS2 Art.21 contains no explicit attack-detection system requirement; §31(2) BSIG gold-plates by mandating that critical infrastructure operators deploy continuous automated attack detection systems meeting state-of-the-art technical standards — this is a German superset obligation with no NIS2 equivalent.

NIS2 Arts.20-23 do not mandate entity registration with a national authority; §33 BSIG gold-plates by requiring all besonders wichtige and wichtige Einrichtungen to register with BSI within three months, providing contact details, sector classification, and IP ranges — purely a German addition.

NIS2 Art.21 requires measures but does not mandate periodic proof submission to a national authority; §39 BSIG gold-plates by requiring Betreiber kritischer Anlagen to submit audit/certification evidence every three years covering §30(1), §31(1), and §31(2) measures to BSI — a German superset obligation.

Art.23(6) explicitly requires authorities to preserve the entity's security interests and commercial confidentiality when sharing cross-border incident information; §32(5) BSIG implements authority-to-authority forwarding without a corresponding explicit confidentiality preservation clause for forwarded incident data — Germany does not reproduce this sub-obligation explicitly.

Art.23(5) requires CSIRT to offer guidance or operational advice on mitigation measures upon request; §32(6) BSIG empowers BSI to offer support (Unterstützungsangebote) to reporting entities for incident remediation — equivalent in substance, Germany makes support a BSI discretionary offer rather than a request-triggered obligation.

Art.22(1) explicitly addresses both technical and non-technical risk factors (e.g. foreign state interference) in supply chain assessments at Union level; §30(2) Nr.4 BSIG focuses on direct supplier security relationships without explicitly addressing the non-technical/geopolitical risk dimension that Art.22 elevates — NIS2 is broader at this sub-point.

Art.21(2)(g) separates 'basic cyber hygiene practices' from 'cybersecurity training' as two elements; §30(2) Nr.7 BSIG combines both as 'grundlegende Schulungen und Sensibilisierungsmaßnahmen' — the hygiene practices element is subsumed in awareness/training, content is co-extensive even if articulation slightly differs.

Art.21(2)(j) specifically includes 'secured emergency communication systems within the entity'; §30(2) Nr.10 BSIG explicitly adds 'gesicherte Notfallkommunikationssysteme' as a distinct element alongside MFA and voice/video/text communications — direct equivalence including the emergency comms sub-element.
